:_mod-docs-content-type: CONCEPT
[id="kms-encryption-about_{context}"]
= About Kubernetes KMS encryption

[role="_abstract"]
Kubernetes Key Management Service (KMS) encryption provides an envelope encryption mechanism for securing etcd data. Instead of encrypting data directly with a single key stored in the cluster, KMS uses a two-tier key hierarchy:

* *Data Encryption Key (DEK)*: A key used to encrypt the actual data stored in etcd. This key is generated by {product-title}.
* *Key Encryption Key (KEK)*: A key managed by an external KMS provider that encrypts the DEK. The KEK never leaves the KMS provider.

When you enable KMS encryption, {product-title} sends the DEK to the external KMS provider for encryption. The encrypted DEK is stored alongside the encrypted data in etcd. When data needs to be decrypted, {product-title} sends the encrypted DEK to the KMS provider, which decrypts it and returns the plain text DEK.

This approach provides several security benefits:

* The KEK is managed externally and never exposed to the cluster
* You can use hardware security modules (HSMs) or cloud-based KMS services
* Centralized key management and auditing through your KMS provider
* Enhanced compliance with regulatory requirements

[NOTE]
====
KMS encryption encrypts the same resources as standard etcd encryption: Secrets, ConfigMaps, Routes, OAuth access tokens, and OAuth authorize tokens. It does not encrypt resource types, namespaces, or object names.
====
